hook: You already know that a circle is defined as all points equidistant from a center, and you can find circumference and area with ease. But what happens when line segments cut through or touch a circle? A chord, a secant, a tangent, a radius, a diameter — these all have precise names and special properties that unlock powerful theorems later in the unit. Can you identify which segment must always pass through the center, and which one can only touch the circle at exactly one point?
concept: Lines and segments in circles are the building blocks for nearly every circle theorem. A circle can be intersected by lines in different ways, and each way has a specific name. Formally: a **radius** is a segment from the center to any point on the circle; a **diameter** is a chord through the center; a **chord** is any segment with both endpoints on the circle; a **secant** is a line that intersects the circle at exactly two points; and a **tangent** is a line that touches the circle at exactly one point, called the point of tangency.
anatomy: Every named segment or line in a circle has a distinct role. The **center** $O$ is the fixed reference point from which the radius $r$ is measured. A **radius** $\overline{OA}$ runs from $O$ to a point $A$ on the circle, so its length is always $r$. A **diameter** $\overline{AB}$ passes through $O$ and has length $2r$, making it the longest possible chord. A **chord** $\overline{CD}$ connects any two points on the circle but need not pass through $O$. A **secant** is the full line (or ray) extending through a chord, intersecting the circle at two points. A **tangent** line touches the circle at exactly one point and is always perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point of tangency.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Circle } O \text{ with radius } r = 7. \text{ Diameter } \overline{AB} \text{ passes through } O.
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying the given information: the circle has center $O$ and radius $r = 7$. Because a diameter must pass through the center and connect two points on the circle, segment $\overline{AB}$ is the longest chord possible. Recognizing which segment is a diameter versus a plain chord is the first skill to lock in.

The diameter is always exactly twice the radius, so $d = 2r$. We substitute $r = 7$ to get $d = 14$. This relationship is foundational: every time you see a diameter, you can immediately split it into two radii of equal length.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Chord } \overline{CD}: C \text{ and } D \text{ on circle } O, \overline{CD} \text{ does not pass through } O.
worked_example.description_latex: Now suppose we draw a different segment whose endpoints $C$ and $D$ both lie on the circle, but the segment does not pass through $O$. This is a chord — it is shorter than the diameter and has no obligation to go through the center. The key test: both endpoints on the circle, but center not required on the segment.

Finally, if a line touches the circle at exactly one point $P$, it is a tangent. The critical property is that the tangent is always perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency, meaning $\ell \perp \overline{OP}$. This perpendicularity fact will be used constantly when solving for unknown lengths in later theorems.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Circle } O, \text{ radius } r = 5. \text{ Point } T \text{ on the circle; tangent line } \ell \text{ at } T.
partial_example.description_latex: We are given a circle with center $O$ and radius $5$, and a tangent line $\ell$ touching the circle at point $T$. Our goal is to find the relationship between $\overline{OT}$ and line $\ell$, and to state the length of $\overline{OT}$.
Since $T$ is on the circle, segment $\overline{OT}$ is a radius, so its length equals $r = 5$. This step is done for you — notice we are simply applying the definition of radius.

State the angle relationship between the tangent line $\ell$ and the radius $\overline{OT}$ at point $T$. Write the relationship using proper notation and explain in one sentence WHY this must be true based on the definition of a tangent.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A circle has radius } r = 9. \text{ What is the length of its diameter?}

\text{Name every type of segment or line shown: (a) a segment from center } O \text{ to point } A \text{ on the circle, (b) a segment connecting two points on the circle that passes through } O, \text{ (c) a segment connecting two points on the circle that does NOT pass through } O.
\text{Tangent } \overline{PT} \text{ touches circle } O \text{ at } T. \text{ If } OT = 6 \text{ and } OP = 10, \text{ find } PT.
\text{Chord } \overline{AB} \text{ has length } 16 \text{ and is bisected perpendicularly by radius } \overline{OM}. \text{ If the radius of the circle is } 10, \text{ find } OM.

\text{A ladder of length } 26 \text{ ft leans against a cylindrical water tower of radius } 10 \text{ ft. The base of the ladder just touches the outside of the tower (tangent point). How far is the base of the ladder from the center of the tower's circular cross-section?}

common_mistakes: Students often call any segment inside a circle a 'radius,' forgetting that a radius must start at the center. A chord with both endpoints on the circle but not passing through the center is NOT a radius — check whether one endpoint is the center before using that label.

Students confuse 'secant' and 'chord': a chord is a segment (finite, both endpoints on the circle), while a secant is the full line or ray that extends through those two points beyond the circle. Using chord length formulas on secant problems (or vice versa) leads to setup errors.

When applying the tangent-radius perpendicularity, students sometimes draw the right angle at the wrong vertex — they place 90° at the center $O$ instead of at the point of tangency $T$. The perpendicular relationship is always $\overline{OT} \perp \ell$ AT point $T$, so the right angle belongs at $T$.
untested_variants: Two tangent segments drawn from the same external point to a circle and proving they are equal in length (the Two-Tangent Theorem).
Finding the length of a chord given its distance from the center using the relationship $\text{half-chord}^2 + d^2 = r^2$ when the perpendicular distance $d$ is not a whole-number Pythagorean triple.
Identifying and distinguishing secant-secant, secant-tangent, and tangent-tangent angle relationships formed outside or inside the circle (arc-angle theorems).
Problems where the diameter is given and students must work backwards to find a chord length using the inscribed angle theorem combined with chord properties.
